title: Upgrading to Windows XP: A Long and Winding Road
author: Peter S. Kastner
source_file: 2001 Upgrading to Windows XP_ A Long and Winding Road PSK.pdf
abstract: Published at the launch of Windows XP in November 2001, this Aberdeen InSight documents Peter Kastner's firsthand experience upgrading PCs to Windows XP. The study concludes that upgrading existing machines is advisable only for skilled technicians, and recommends that most consumers and small businesses obtain Windows XP pre-installed on a new PC at approximately $1,000. The study is notable for evaluating XP's wireless networking support (Agere/ORiNOCO), media capabilities, and SOHO applicability.
importance_rationale: Published on the day of Windows XP's consumer launch alongside a $200 million Microsoft marketing campaign, this first-person field study provided rare critical independent guidance at a time when upgrade enthusiasm was high. Aberdeen's cautious recommendation carried significant weight with enterprise IT decision-makers.
relevance_rationale: Windows XP reached end-of-support in April 2014; the specific upgrade guidance is obsolete. However the methodology — firsthand upgrade testing with clear consumer recommendations — remains a useful model for OS migration advisories.
prescience_rationale: The prediction that most users should buy new XP hardware rather than upgrade proved entirely correct; the retail upgrade path was abandoned by most major PC makers in subsequent OS cycles, and XP itself ran successfully on new hardware for over a decade before EOL in 2014.
